coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently received much attention due to their superior properties, such as quantum-sized, strong light absorption, tunable luminescence with high quantum yield, excellent ability to donate and accept electrons, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness. CDs have been applied in energy conversion and storage, photovoltaic, biomedical, environmental treatment, etc. Note that with the rapid development in the field of environmental treatment and protection, the recently updated CDs synthesis and applications are extremely urgent. In this progress report, we sketch recent achievements in the synthesis and applications of CDs in the field of environmental treatment and protection, including pollutant detection, adsorption, degradation, solar-to-steam generation system, separation membranes, and antibacterial and antifungal. Furthermore, future opportunities and difficulties in developing CDs applications in solving environmental issues are also discussed.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The properties of CDs and its applications in environmental treatment and protection.
The pollutant detection application of carbon dots.

(a) The adsorption capacity of CDs/ZnFe2O4 for methyl orange, (b) The methyl blue removal efficiency of LDH-CDs and (c) The mechanism of the malachite green, direct blue 5, and congo red adsorption on CDs@MgAl-LDO [92–94]. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Photocatalytic performance under visible light and the electron transfer of (a) CDs as a photocatalyst, (b) GQDs/TNS as photosensitizer and electron donor, and (c) CDs/ZnO as photosensitizer and electron acceptor, and (d) CDs as photosensitizer and electron bridge [96–99].

Recombination processes of photogenerated excitons for (a) single carbon dot and (b) self-assembled CDs [67]. (c) N,S,P-CDs as a volumetric system and (d) Carbonized carbon dots-modified starch aerogel as interfacial systems [108–110].
The application of CDs in membrane and the salt rejection performance of CD-based (a) reverse osmosis, (b) forward osmosis, and (c) nanofiltration membrane [114–117].
Advantages and disadvantages of CDs synthesis methods.

row.entry: Fast synthesis with controllable morphology and size, non-using harmful chemicals.
The complicated operation, high cost, low yield, low quantum yield (QY), and non-suitable for large-scale synthesis.
With fast synthesis, cheap cost, and controllable size, the products have good stability and no agglomeration in high-salt concentration solutions.
Simple, cost-effective, suitable for large-scale manufacturing, no elaborate equipment, and able to synthesize from biomass materials.
Long synthesis duration, toxic reagents, requires strong acid/base
With easy operation, eco-friendly high yield, and high QY, the products have good stability and no agglomeration in high salt concentration solutions.
Make noise, high energy cost, long synthesis
Easy operation, fast synthesis, uniform size distribution, high QY, easy size control, eco-friendly, ability to synthesize from biomass materials
Easy operation, solvent-free approach, wide precursor tolerance, fast reaction time, low cost, large-scale production, ability to synthesize from biomass materials
Non-uniform size distribution, broad size distribution
Simple, various starting materials, large scale production, high QY, high yield, able to synthesize from biomass materials
High QY, high yield, low cost, various starting materials, non-toxic, ecologically friendly, well-control nucleation processes, able to synthesize from biomass materials.
Fast synthesis, well-controls the size, less toxic, able to synthesize from biomass materials, and easy production on a large scale.
The complicated operation, low QY, expensive equipment, and long synthesis duration.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect mechanism of several factors on photocatalytic degradation.

row.entry: An excitation source with enough energy can lead to the generation of electrons in the conduction band and holes in the valence band. They can then react with O2 and H2O to form reactive oxygen species (ROS). As a result, ROS can efficiently degrade organic pollutants.
Distance between light source and photocatalyst
The closer the distance between the light source and the CDs, the more photons are received. As a result, the formation of photoexcited holes and electrons takes place faster and in greater amounts.
Under irradiation, the amount of ROS is proportional to the reaction time. Therefore, the pollutant degradation efficiency will increase over time.

(1) High concentrations of pollutants may prevent the exposure of light (photons) to the surface of CDs. In other words, they slow down or block the progress of the photocatalytic processes.
(2) The form of pollutant in the aqueous and the surface charge of CDs affect the binding and adhesion between them. As a result, pollutant decomposition occurs at a low rate if repulsion force prevails, and vice versa.
(3) Contaminants with large and complex structures can be degraded into intermediates. Therefore, more decomposition time may be required to achieve high removal efficiency.

(1) More CDs provide a larger active site, electrons, and holes. Therefore, the degradation rate increases when the dose concentration increases.
(2) The exposure between the material and the light decreases when the CDs concentration exceeds the saturation level.
The reaction temperature affects the adsorption-desorption ability of the photocatalyst.
pH affects the surface charge of CDs, the electrostatic interactions between the pollutants and the photocatalysts, and the formation and ionization states of the pollutant.

(1) e− can react with dissolved oxygen in the solution to form superoxide radicals.
(2) Dissolved oxygen can strongly adsorb UV photons at 185 and 254 nm. In this case, dissolved oxygen acts as the UV filter. As a result, light intensity decreases.

(1) Inorganic ions can compete with pollutants in the adsorption and degradation processes.
(2) Inorganic ions can prevent the exposure of photons and CDs.
(3) Anions such as chloride, carbonate, phosphate, and sulfate can react with both e−, h+, and OH⁎.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have recently received much attention due to their superior properties, such as quantum-sized, strong light absorption, tunable luminescence with high quantum yield, excellent ability to donate and accept electrons, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental friendliness. CDs have been applied in energy conversion and storage, photovoltaic, biomedical, environmental treatment, etc. Note that with the rapid development in the field of environmental treatment and protection, the recently updated CDs synthesis and applications are extremely urgent. In this progress report, we sketch recent achievements in the synthesis and applications of CDs in the field of environmental treatment and protection, including pollutant detection, adsorption, degradation, solar-to-steam generation system, separation membranes, and antibacterial and antifungal. Furthermore, future opportunities and difficulties in developing CDs applications in solving environmental issues are also discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of society leads to the emergence of more and more environmental issues, such as environmental pollution by toxic substances, water scarcity, etc. Therefore, the urgent problem is finding solutions for environmental issues and improving environmental quality. Advanced material applications are one of the most effective methods for environmental protection. The strong development of nanomaterials, including metallic-based, carbon-based, and metal-organic framework (MOF)-based nanoparticles, has been recognized in the first two decades of the 21st century. The synthesized materials have been successfully applied in environmental treatment and protection. Note that carbon-based materials, such as activated carbon, graphite, CNT, etc., are the most commonly used in this field. This is due to a low cost, environmental friendliness, high specific surface areas with large pore volumes, excellent stability, and wide range of synthesis precursors.

Carbon dots (CDs) were first discovered in 2004, and since then, they have received a lot of attention [1]. This is due to their superior advantages, such as excellent fluorescence, bio-compatibility, environmental friendliness, no photobleaching, and ease of production. CDs are also considered to be a representative of the group of non-dimensional nanomaterials. CDs exhibit several properties that are superior to those of other carbon-based materials, including (1) superior water and organic solvents dispersibility due to the variety of surface functional groups [2]; (2) unique optoelectronic properties, such as tunable luminescence and good photostability [3,4]; and (3) effortless donating and accepting electrons, which has been clearly demonstrated in the pollutant detection and degradation applications by CD-based photocatalysts [5,6] (Fig. 1 ). Therefore, the synthesis and application experiments of CDs and CD-based materials have been carried out in a wide range of fields such as solar to steam generation (SSG) [7–9], photovoltaic [10,11], biomedical [12,13], environmental treatment [14–17], photocatalysis [18], etc.

Several overviews of the use of CDs in the detection and removal of pollutants applications have been published [19–21]. For example, Long's group summarized the CDs applications in environmental pollution control, including environmental pollution sensing, contaminant adsorption, membrane separation, pollutant degradation, and antimicrobial [22]. Rani and co-workers discussed the properties of the carbon quantum dots and their application in wastewater treatment and the biomedical field [23]. However, with the rapid development in this field, the recently updated CDs synthesis and applications are extremely urgent.

In this progress report, we sketch recent achievements of CDs applications in the field of environmental treatment and protection, including pollutant detection, pollutant adsorption, organic pollutant photodegradation, SSG, separation membranes, antibacterial, and antifungal. Firstly, we outlined the synthesis methods and properties of CDs for environmental treatment and protection applications. Secondly, the recent progress in the applications of CDs in the field of environmental treatment and protection is carefully discussed. Finally, the prospects and challenges of the development of CDs in applications to protect the environment are also discussed.

The precursor plays the most important role in the formation of CDs. Several commercial chemical materials, such as o-Phenylenediamine, sulfamide, polyethylene glycol, oxalic acid, dicyandiamide, gallic acid, phosphoric acid, 3-aminophenyl boronic acid, basic fuchsin, citric acid, etc. have been used in synthesizing CDs. Besides commercial chemical materials, waste (waste paper, cow manure, vehicle exhaust soot, etc.), natural compounds, and biomass (eggs, leaves, wool, etc.) have also been used as precursors for CDs synthesis. Hydrothermal method, solvothermal technique, pyrolysis, microwave-assisted process, and chemical oxidation method are suitable methods for synthesizing CDs from natural compounds. Moreover, the impurities in these precursors, such as sulfur, nitrogen, phosphorus, etc., can contribute to the improvement of the properties of CDs. Remarkably, CDs particles can be decomposed under white fluorescent light [63]. Although the photodecomposition takes a relatively long time, the products are potentially toxic to both normal and cancerous human cells. Thus, to reduce the self-decomposition of CDs and the use of chemicals or solvents, natural product-based CDs should be used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of carbon dots in environmental treatment

ce:section.ce:para: The CD-based pollutant detection is based on the optical and catalytic properties and the interaction between the pollutant and CDs, leading to the emission being quenched or changing the intensity and position of the emission peak. It is found that the main quenching mechanisms of CD-based fluorescence sensors includesfluorescence quenching, dynamic quenching effects, static quenching effects (SQE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and surface energy transfer (SET). On the other hand, based on the mechanism, CD-based sensors are also classified into two groups namelyfluorescence sensors and colorimetric sensors (Fig. 3 ).

On the experimental scale, CDs show the detectability of toxic pollutants in the aquatic environment with different typesincluding metal cations (Fe3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Cr6+, …), several anions (S2−, NO2 −, …), bacteria (E. coli, Salmonella), and other compounds (pesticides, antibiotics, …) and with different detection limit ranging from ppb to ppm. Notably, CDs and CD-based composites can be used to detect radioactive ions [64–68]. For instance, uranyl ions (UO2 2+) can be detected by N-doped CDs with a low detection limit of 20.38 nM [64]. 2-Thenoyltrifluoroacetone-CDs-aerogel was fabricated by Dolai's group, which showed selective and sensitive sensing capabilities for UO2 2+, Sm3+, and Eu3+ ions [67]. Several factors, such as the concentration of CDs, the composition of the medium, the characteristics of the pollutant, the wavelength and intensity of the excitation light, and the spectrometer parameter settings, affect the determined PL spectral intensity and also the sensitivity of both types of sensors. Besides, CDs synthesized from biomass materials by hydrothermal, solvothermal, and microwave methods have a high ability of light absorption and excellent luminescence, which is appropriate for pollutant detection applications. Surface modification of CDs or doping with heteroatoms (N, S, P, etc.) proved to improve efficiency in pollutant detection of CDs rather than improve crystallinity. Table 2 shows some pollutant detection limits of CDs and types of sensing used in the respective studies.

In recent years, the detection of pollutants based on CDs has been extended to other environments, such as detecting alachlor and trifluralin herbicides in the soil and detecting nitrogen dioxide, and methane in the air [83–86]. Moreover, CDs have also been shown to be an effective probe in detecting many toxins that exist in foods for human consumption. For example, Chen and co-workers successfully synthesized N, S/CQDs through hydrothermal treatment of crayfish shells, which are food wastes with no value [87]. N, S/CQDs were then used as a fluorescence probe to detect toxically. Thiamphenicol detection limits were determined to be 11.12 and 27.5 μg/L in water and real food (fish and milk), respectively. In addition, the obtained results reflected extremely low cytotoxicity, and it can be used to detect TAP in living Hela cells. Note that pollutant detection is one of the most notable applications of CDs.

Their majority of properties, such as tiny size, high specific surface area, good water dispersibility, tunable surface functional groups, and surface chemistry properties, show that CDs are promising adsorbents. Moreover, a large number of negatively charged surface groups, such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxylic groups, create an effective electrostatic interaction with cationic pollutants as well as provide active adsorption sites for the adsorption. Indeed, CDs without modification are effective adsorbents for Pb2+ treatment [14]. After 260 min of adsorption, the CDs illustrated up to 80.6 % Pb2+ removal efficiency, which is higher than several other materials [88,89]. CDs derived from frying oil or grass have been shown to remove Ni2+ and Cd2+ from water [90,91]. However, CDs often demonstrate extremely low efficiency in anionic or organic pollutant adsorption due to the electrostatic repulsion between them.

Combination with other materials, such as layered double hydroxide (LDH), ZnFe2O4, mesoporous silica, etc., is a common and effective method to enhance cationic and anionic pollutant removal efficiency [92–94]. For example, due to improving the electrostatic attraction and Van der Waals force between adsorbent and adsorbate, methyl orange adsorption capacity increased from 75.5 to 109.7 mg/g with the increase of CDs from 0 to 5 % in CDs/ZnFe2O4 composite [93]. Ge et al. successfully synthesized layered double oxides hybrid coated with carbon dots (CDs@MgAl-LDO) via a two-step procedure, including hydrothermal method to form SDBS@MgAl-LDH (SDBS as carbon source) and calcination method to form CDs@MgAl-LDO [94]. In comparison with other as-prepared materials, CDs@MgAl-LDO exhibited more negatively charged oxygen-containing groups, a larger BET-specific surface area, and a higher total pore volume. Moreover, CDs also reduced wettability and increased the affinity of the composites for organic pollutants. As a result, CDs@MgAl-LDO showed outstanding efficiencies for the removal of both cationic and anionic dyes, including malachite green, direct blue 5, and congo red. Electrostatic attraction, hydrophobic interaction, surface complexation, and hydrogen bonding between CDs@MgAl-LDO and adsorbates were the adsorption mechanisms (Fig. 4c). In another study, there was no adsorption of methyl blue on CDs while LDH showed a 45 % removal efficiency (Fig. 4b) [92]. In contrast, the efficiency of LDH-CDs reached 96 % within 20 min of treatment, which was higher than the former materials. This is attributed to the simultaneous formation of the electrostatic force and the hydrogen bonding between LDH, CDs, and methyl blue. CD-based LDH materials not only efficiently adsorbed dyes but also had very high maximum adsorption capacities for Hg2+ and Ag+ of 625 and 714.3 mg/g, respectively [95]. Therefore, CDs and CD-based composites have great potential for pollutant adsorption. Besides, natural resources-based CDs can be considered as natural adsorbents, which are always appreciated more than artificial adsorbents. However, the influence of environmental factors (such as pH, temperature, the presence of anions, and cations, etc.), the stability and reusability of CDs, as well as the desorption of pollutants, are still issues that need to be researched to be applied in practice.

In the last few years, photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants by CDs has received much attention. The mechanism of this process is based on the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as ⁎OH, ⁎O2 −, h+ vb (Fig. 5a). The rate and product of photocatalytic degradation by CDs and CD-based materials are highly dependent on the excitation energy source, reaction time, contaminant concentration and nature, amount of photocatalyst used, reaction temperature, pH of the medium, oxygen concentration in water, and presence of inorganic ions. The effect mechanisms of the above factors on photocatalytic degradation are described in Table 3 .

CDs can play many different roles in photocatalyst systems, such as an independent photocatalyst material, a photosensitizer, or as an electron donor or acceptor. For example, due to the up-conversion photoluminescence properties, CDs were decorated on the surface of wide band gap semiconducting materials such as ZnO that can absorb visible or near-infrared light (λ1) and then emit ultraviolet photons (λ2) to activate the photocatalyst system (Fig. 5b) [98]. Besides, CDs presented the role of an electron acceptor in this research. CDs also presented a similar role in the CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst system, according to the research of Deng et al. [100]. Note that there is no photocatalytic ability of CDs for methyl orange (MO). However, the degradation ability of MO was highly enhanced with the CDs/TiO2 sample [100]. Specifically, under 6 h of irradiation with UV and visible light, respectively, 78.75 % and 70.56 % of MO with an initial concentration of 40 ppm were degraded by CQDs/TiO2.

There are a lot of works on photocatalytic degradation by CDs with various toxic organic pollutants such as dyes, pharmaceutical substances, etc. Under optimal conditions (pH = 2, the amount of used photocatalyst material is 0.1 g/L and under UV lamp irradiation, treatment time of 80 min), 70 % of imipramine presented in the wastewater from the Parsian pharmaceuticals company were degraded by CDs [101]. In addition, COD decreased from 600 mg/L to 345 mg/L after treatment. Similarly, the maximum methylene blue removal efficiency was achieved at 87 % with a degradation rate of 0.021 min−1 using CDs at a concentration of 6.67 mg/L [96]. This indicates the non-selectivity for organics of CD-based photocatalytic oxidation.

Moreover, CDs are easily combined with some metals, semiconductors, etc. to enhance the activity of the catalyst system. Ogi et al. synthesized CDs by a microwave-combined hydrothermal method using citric acid and urea as synthetic precursors [102]. There was a strong absorption peak at 650 nm due to a large number of pyrrolic N surface functional groups [102]. This absorption band shift has great significance for photocatalytic decomposition since most of the solar energy is concentrated in the visible light region. By a simple deposition method, CDs particles were mounted by Dong's group on the titanium thin plate (TNS) surface [97]. As a result, there was an enhancement of light absorption in the visible region (λ > 420 nm) while only a slight change in the UV region was observed. At the same time, under the effect of excitation light in the visible region, the electrons at the HOMO level of the CDs adsorb the energy and jump to the LUMO energy level, leaving a photogenic hole at the HOMO level (Fig. 5c). The generated photoelectrons in the LUMO region of CDs easily continue to move to the conduction band of the TNS. This process limits the recombination between the photogenerated electrons and holes of CDs. As a result, the number of ROS generated by CDs/TNS was larger than those of CDs and TNS, leading to superior RhB degradation efficiency of CDs/TNS.

CDs can also act as bridges for the transfer of electrons between different semiconductors such as g-C3N4/CDs/AgBr, α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 (Fig. 5d). This increases the interfacial contact between components in the photocatalyst system [99,103,104]. There are many advantages of the presence of CDs in the α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 including lattice mismatch, high electron-hole recombination, and weak interfacial contact between components. As a result, the indole degradation efficiency by α-Fe2O3/NCDs/g-C3N4 is higher than those of α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3/g-C3N4, NCDs, and NCDs/g-C3N4.

In terms of applicability as an independent photocatalyst, CDs have lower pollutant degradation efficiency, but it exhibits strong light absorption in the full range of solar compared to TiO2. The absorption peak of CDs can be shifted from the UV to the VIS and NIR regions through surface modification [102,105,106]. This is extremely beneficial for most photocatalyst applications. Furthermore, CDs can act in different roles in the photocatalyst system to enhance pollutant removal efficiency. Therefore, the combination of CDs and semiconductors is expected to obtain efficient photocatalyst materials.

Due to their strong broadband sunlight absorbability, CDs are one of the promising materials for solar-to-steam generation applications. It is considered a new method of solving the problem of freshwater scarcity that is occurring in many regions of the world.

In particular, CDs and CD-based materials have been applied in both types of solar-to-steam generation systems (SSG), including volumetric systems and interfacial systems as shown in Fig. 6 . Note that the water evaporation rates under 0 and 1 sun illumination are calculated by expression of

projected is the projected area (m2) [107]. Fig. 6a and b present the recombination processes of photogenerated excitons for single CD and the self-assembled CDs [67]. The photogenerated electron/hole transfers could occur in heterostructures and suppress radiative recombination in CDs (Fig. 6a), resulting in the nonradiative recombination of photoinduced electrons. This phenomenon causes fluorescence quenching and releases heat (Fig. 6b). Hence, the assembly of CDs could enhance the efficiency of converting solar energy into heat, leading to a higher performance of SSG system.

Based on this concept, N,S,P-CDs were synthesized by Chattopadhyay et al. and dispersed in water at a concentration of 80 mg/ml to form a volumetric evaporation system [108]. The results showed that under 1 W/cm2-intensity simulated solar illumination, the evaporation rate of the nanofluid reached 28.9 mL dm−3 min−1. To fabricate the interfacial system, CDs film was coated on the substrate by different methods such as blade-casting, dip-coating, and depositing. Due to the self-float and excellent water transport through microchannels of balsa wood, it was chosen as a substrate for CDs interfacial evaporating system in the study of Hou's group [111]. The wood-supported CDs showed stronger optical absorption than that of wood or original CD samples in the full solar spectrum. The evaporation rate was determined to be 2.27 kg m−2 h−1 under solar illumination of 1 kWm−2, 3 times higher than that of balsa wood. Similarly, CDs@cellulose paper had an evaporation rate of 2.31 kg m−2 h−1 under 1kWm−2 illumination, 7.2 times higher than the evaporation rate of pure water [112]. In another way, the CD-based aerogel with a 3D interconnected porous structure was fabricated to a new solar evaporator [110]. It showed a stable evaporation rate of 2.29 kg m−2 h−1 under 1 sun irradiation, even in harsh conditions. Noticeably, the crystallinity of the material shows a greater influence than the amount of doping with heteroatoms, transition metals, etc. in the solar steam generation application. Nguyen's group synthesized CDs/Fluorophores hybrid by drying the product solution obtained from the hydrothermal synthesis of CDs and reported its application in SSG [113]. According to this research, a high evaporation rate of 2.1 kg m−2 h−1 was obtained for the developed solar-absorbing material. On the other hand, the application of CDs for steam generation and water recovery from polluted water and salt water can also be considered one of the effective methods of pollutant treatment and desalination [111]. Furthermore, CDs synthesis usually has a byproduct of carbon spheres, which are larger than carbon dots and can be obtained by centrifugation or filtration. In some cases, samples with a pore at micrometer size even have a higher evaporation efficiency than samples with a nanometer pore size. As reported by Dao and Choi, the cause of this phenomenon is water trapped when moving through extremely narrow channels [7]. In summary, it should be further research on the applicability of CDs in a SSG system and by-products in the CD synthesis process.

Due to the good biocompatibility, ultra-small size, non-toxic, highly hydrophilic, abundant surface functional groups, stable photoluminescence, fast proton conductivity, excellent light capture capability, and other outstanding physicochemical properties, much research has been carried out to evaluate the applicability of CDs in filtration membranes in recent years, such as forward osmosis (FO), reverse osmosis (RO), organic solvent nanofiltration, nanofiltration, etc. as shown in Fig. 7 . Note that the overall performance of membranes significantly is enhanced with the integration of membranes with CDs (CDs@membranes). The presence of CDs can enhance the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface, changing the morphology, roughness, thickness, and density of the structure of the selective layer of the membrane separation. In order to prepare CDs@membranes, three methods can be utilized [118]. The first method is grafting, which is the process of coating the original membrane surface with various coating agents, such as dopamine and (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane. The second one is doping, which is the incorporation of CDs into polymers. The last one is layer-by-layer self-assembly, which is a method to fabricate multifunctional composite membranes by sequential adsorption of multilayer materials through electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding interactions, and coordination bonding interactions. The CDs/polyamide thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) RO membrane on the microporous polysulfone support was prepared by interfacial polymerization of monomers of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimethyl chloride (TMC) [114]. The results showed a filtration efficiency of up to 99.0 % of the salt removed with an initial NaCl concentration of 2000 mg/L. Moreover, the water flux and stability of RO membranes in the presence of CDs were also significantly increased. The water flux was 88.7 L m−2 h−1 with 0.02 wt% CDs and a coating time of 6 min. By almost the same method, graphene quantum dot (GQD)-incorporated thin film composite (TFC) was fabricated by Xu et al. [115]. The water flux of an FO membrane using GQD-incorporated TFC was 12.9 L m−2 h−1, while a comparable reverse salt flux of 1.41 g m−2 h−1 (experiment with DI water and 0.5 M MgCl2). It was proved that CDs in polyethyleneimine (PEI) matrix with TMC and polyacrylonitrile support were efficient organic solvent nanofiltration membranes in the research of Yuan et al. [119]. Due to the existence of hydrophilic surface groups, CDs allowed the transport of polar solvents and prevented the adsorption of non-polar solvents. However, the effect of CDs on the performance of filtration membranes is still unclear. This might provide ideas for future research directions, advancing the application of CD-based membranes.

CDs have been reported to kill or inhibit the growth of microbes. The primary mechanism is that under irradiation-excited conditions, reactive oxygen species are formed. They then adhere to and break down cell walls and membranes, causing DNA damage, changing the structure of genetically structured cells, etc. [123]. As mentioned above, CDs can be synthesized from natural precursors. In some cases, compounds with antibacterial activity from the herbs-based precursors were retained on CDs, thereby enhancing their ability to inhibit fungi and bacteria (Fig. 8 ). For example, A. Gedanken et al. used turmeric leaves (Curcuma longa) to synthesize carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment [120]. As a result, the CDs were outstandingly effective in inhibiting the growth of some common bacteria, such as E. coli and S. aureus, due to the support of phytochemicals (demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin) on the surface or inside the CDs structure. Minimum inhibitory concentrations were determined to be 0.25 mg/mL for E. coli and S. aureus and 0.5 mg/mL for K. pneumoniae and S. epidermidis. Apart from Curcuma longa, many other herbs have been successfully used as precursors for CDs synthesis, such as Alisma, herbal tea, Pinellia ternataas, Osmanthus fragrans, mustard seeds, Phellodendri Chinensis Cortex, etc. [124–129]. However, their antibacterial activities have yet to be evaluated. In addition, many studies have been published on antifungal activity (R. solani, P. grisea, C. albicans, …) [130–132]. It can be seen that, with their adsorption and photocatalytic ability, CDs with antibacterial and antifungal properties have great potential for application in wastewater treatment, especially in domestic wastewater with high chemical oxygen demand and high concentrations of pathogenic microorganisms.

According to this research, CDs have a high ability to apply in environmental treatment. However, several challenges and future directions could be listed below:
–
CDs can be synthesized by bottom-up and top-down methods. However, the by-products of CDs synthesis have not been considered specifically.
–
The methods of separating and recovering CDs after applying in environmental treatment such as pollutant detection, and pollutant adsorption should be futher studied.
–
Due to ultranano size, CDs can be lost along with water evaporation or water flowing through the membrane in the solar steam generation application.
–
Studies on the effect of CDs concentration, as well as absorption layer thickness on evaporation efficiency have not been performed.
–
CDs@membranes can improve filtration performance. However, the research on effect of CDs on the performance of some types of membranes is still limit.
–
Evaluation of the influence of environmental factors on the adsorption and photocatalytic reaction efficiency as well as bactericidal and antifungal activities of CDs would be underwent in the future.
–
Single applications of CDs in environmental treatment are reported in many studies. However, investigation of the combination of simultaneous applications of CDs is limited.

In this report, we focus on recent advances in applying CDs to environmental treatment and protection, including pollutant detection, adsorption and degradation, SSG, and separation membranes, and antibacterial and antifungal. Apart from the unique properties of original CDs, the introduction of the heteroatoms (N, S, P, etc.) as surface groups also plays an important role in the application of CDs in pollutant detection and degradation, solar-to-steam generation, or creating bond with heavy metal pollutants in the adsorption process. Microwave, pyrolysis, hydrothermal, and solvothermal methods are appreciated methods to synthesis CDs applied in environment field due to ease of setup, controllable CDs size, and environmentally friendly synthesis process using natural materials as precursors. There are many difficulties and challenges for development CDs in environmental treatment and protection applications, resulting in several future directions.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tran Nam Anh received a bachelor's degree and a master's degree in environmental science at Hanoi University of Science in 2018 and 2021, respectively. He is currently a Ph.D. student at the VNU University of Science under the co-supervision of Dr. Van-Duong Dao and Dr. Nguyen Thi Hanh. His Ph.D. research concentrates on hybrid materials and their application to solar cells. His research also focuses on pollutant treatment and environmental protection.

Nguyen Thi Hien graduated with an engineering degree in Environmental Engineering at Thuy Loi University in 2019. She is currently working as a researcher at a private institute of the environment. At the same time, she is pursuing a Master's program in environmental sciences at the VNU University of Science under the co-supervision of Dr. Van-Duong Dao and Dr. Thi-Hanh Nguyen. Her research interests include advanced oxidation in water treatment, biological treatment of wastewater, photocatalyst materials, and photothermal materials, … Her master's research concentrates on photothermal materials and their application to freshwater generation.

Nguyen Minh Hoang received a Bachelor's degree in Physics at Hanoi University of Science in 2016. He is currently a Ph.D. student at VNU University of Science under the co-supervision of Dr. Van-Duong Dao and Assoc. Prof. PhD. Le Tuan Tu. His Ph.D. research focuses on hybrid materials and their application in water treatment.

Dang Thi Hai Linh received a Bachelor's degree and a Master's Degree in Environmental Science at the University of Science, VNU University of Science, Hanoi in 2015 and 2018, respectively. She is currently pursuing a Ph.D. program at the VNU University of Science under the co-supervision of Dr. Van-Duong Dao and Prof. Xuan-Co Hoang. Her Ph.D. research concentrates on hybrid materials and their application to solar cells.

Do Quang Huy is currently an Associate Professor and Doctor at the Faculty of Environmental Sciences, University of Science, Vietnam National University, Hanoi (VNU-HUS). He is also the Permanent Vice President cum Secretary General, Head of the Training and Consulting Department, Vietnam Association of Laboratories - Vinalab. He received a Ph.D. in organic chemistry from Vietnam National University in 1992. His research interest focuses on organic chemistry, such as essential oils, chemical composition, natural product chemistry, chemical biology, extraction, etc.

Tran Van Quy is currently an Associate Professor and Doctor at the Faculty of Environmental Sciences, University of Science, Vietnam National University, Hanoi (VNU-HUS). Mr. Quy graduated with an engineering degree in chemical technology from Hanoi University of Science and Technology in 1983. After that, he worked as a researcher at the Center of Materials, Vietnam Atomic Energy Commission. He got his Doctor degree in Environmental Protection and Rational Use of Natural Resources at D. Mendeleyev University of Chemical Technology, Russia (former Soviet Union), in 1994. His major is applying technologies for the suitable use of energy resources. Specifically, he has carried out some related projects and published 1 book as a co-author titled “Potential and Strategy of Using Renewable Energy in Vietnam” in 2009.

Nguyen Thi Hanh received her Ph.D in Chemistry at University of Science, Vietnam National University in 2017. She is currently a Lecturer in the Faculty of Environmental Sciences at VNU University of Science. Her research interests include the development of new materials such as nanocomposites and hybrid nanocomposites, multifunctional materials, and their applications in environmental remediation and green growth.

Vu Ngoc Hung received his Ph. D. in Materials Science and Engineering from Chonnam National University, South Korea in 2018. He is currently a Lecturer in the Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering at Phenikaa University, Vietnam. He is now a member of the Key Lab of Renewable Energy Conversion and Storage (RECS Lab). His research interests include the development of new materials for rechargeable battery and supercapacitor applications and novel synthesis and characterization of nanostructured materials.

Van-Duong Dao is currently a Dean at the Faculty of Biotechnology, Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Phenikaa University, Vietnam. He received his Ph.D. in Chemical Engineering from Chungnam National University, South Korea in 2013. He worked as a Research Professor at the same university, under the support of the Brain Pool program, a prestigious grant from the National Research Foundation, Republic of Korea until 2018. His research mainly focuses on the development of various carbon-based hybrid materials for the application to next-generation solar cells such as dye-sensitized, quantum-dot-sensitized, and perovskite solar cells, solar-to-steam generation, energy storage devices, and photocatalysis. He is currently a Group Leader of the Key Lab of Renewable Energy Conversion and Storage (RECS Lab). Details can be found at: https://recslab.phenikaa-uni.edu.vn/
